hook: You already know that addition and multiplication follow reliable rules — but have you ever wondered *why* those rules hold, or what other systems might obey similar laws? Most of mathematics quietly depends on abstract structures that generalize the arithmetic you've always used, yet those structures remain invisible until you look for them. What do a clock face, a Rubik's cube, and the integers all have in common?
concept: This unit is about recognizing and proving that mathematical systems — sets equipped with operations — can share deep structural similarities, no matter how different they look on the surface. The unifying idea is that of an *algebraic structure*: a set together with operations satisfying specified axioms.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to rigorously verify that a set and operation satisfy closure, associativity, identity, and invertibility — the four axioms that make a group — through careful proof-writing.
Master how to identify subgroups within a group and use cosets to partition a group, building toward Lagrange's theorem and its powerful divisibility consequences.
Apply the concepts of structure-preserving maps to prove when two groups are fundamentally the same, using homomorphisms and isomorphisms to compare algebraic systems.

You will extend the group framework to richer structures with two operations, proving key properties of rings and fields that underpin number systems like $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Q}$, and $\mathbb{R}$.
